items.title: House price regression with one outlier
items.scenario: A linear model predicts house prices. Most errors are within $20k, but one mansion is off by $500k. Training with MSE makes that single example dominate the gradient; switching to MAE reduces the outlier's influence so the model fits typical homes better.
items.explanation: MSE squares errors, so one huge residual can steer training. MAE treats each dollar of error equally — a classic loss-choice trade-off in regression.

items.title: Spam filter binary classification
items.scenario: Label y = 1 (spam), model sigmoid output ŷ = 0.05. BCE = −log(0.05) ≈ 3.0 nats — a steep penalty for assigning low probability to the true class.
items.explanation: BCE is the Bernoulli form of cross-entropy H(P, Q). Confident wrong predictions receive large loss values, pushing the model toward higher q(y_true).

items.title: Digit classifier (10 classes)
items.scenario: True digit 7, softmax gives ŷ = [0.02, 0.01, 0.01, 0.01, 0.01, 0.01, 0.01, 0.90, 0.01, 0.01]. CE = −log(0.90) ≈ 0.11 nats. Same correct argmax with ŷ_7 = 0.55 gives CE ≈ 0.60 — same accuracy, worse probabilistic score.
items.explanation: CE rewards high probability on the true class, not just getting the argmax right. Only ŷ_y enters the one-hot CE formula.

items.title: Training loss vs test RMSE
items.scenario: A team trains a temperature forecaster minimizing MSE on the training set. For stakeholders they report RMSE = √MSE on the held-out test set — same error notion, different scale and role.
items.explanation: MSE is the typical training objective; RMSE is a common evaluation metric. Both measure regression error but serve different stages of the ML workflow.
